description: Machine credentials and secrets that commonly leak into server/application logs: JWTs, AWS access keys, PEM private-key blocks, vendor API tokens with documented fixed prefixes (GitHub, GitLab, Google, Slack, Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid, npm, Anthropic), HTTP Authorization Bearer/Basic credentials, connection-string passwords, and generic key=secret assignments. These tokens are high-entropy and prefix/context-anchored, so the detectors are precise. Replacements are structurally valid for their kind but provably INVALID (they embed the literal REDACTED sentinel, which every detector's `safe` recognises) so a leaked credential is scrubbed before upload, not merely flagged.
notes: All replacements carry the literal 'REDACTED' sentinel (base64url-safe, so even a fake JWT or AKIA key stays shape-valid). Every detector therefore uses safe='REDACTED' for idempotency — the rare real secret that itself contains the substring 'redacted' is simply left alone, which is acceptable.

Vendor-token detectors anchor on the published prefix + length (e.g. ghp_/gho_/ghu_/ghs_/ghr_ + 36, github_pat_ 22_59, glpat- 20, AIza 35, xox[baprs]-, sk_/rk_ live|test, SK + 32 hex, SG. 22.43, npm_ 36, sk-ant-). Their fakes KEEP the prefix so the log still reads like 'a <vendor> token was here'. Publishable/identifier variants are excluded on purpose: Stripe pk_, Twilio AC… account SID.
Bearer credentials that are JWTs are shaped back into a fake JWT; opaque tokens and Basic credentials become an opaque REDACTED token. AWS access keys keep the AKIA/ASIA + 20-char shape.

Entropy-only secrets with NO stable prefix (e.g. an AWS secret access key, generic 32-hex API keys) are deliberately NOT pattern-matched here — they would over-match in logs; the generic key=secret assignment detector plus a downstream review layer cover those.

The generic key=secret assignment detector is the broadest here (matches api_key/secret/token/password/...= '<value>'); it lives in this opt-in profile precisely because that breadth is unwanted by default.

detectors.name: private-key-block
detectors.description: PEM-encoded private key block (RSA/EC/DSA/OpenSSH/PGP/encrypted). The whole block, including the armor lines, is replaced.

detectors.name: jwt
detectors.description: JSON Web Token. The 'eyJ' prefix is base64url of '{"' — a reliable JWT header signal — followed by header.payload(.signature).

detectors.name: aws-access-key
detectors.description: AWS access key id (AKIA long-term / ASIA temporary): a vendor-defined prefix + 16 uppercase-alnum chars, so essentially no false positives.

detectors.name: github-token
detectors.description: GitHub access token: a documented gh{p,o,u,s,r}_ prefix (personal/oauth/user-to-server/server-to-server/refresh) followed by 36 base62 chars.

detectors.name: github-pat-fine
detectors.description: GitHub fine-grained personal access token: github_pat_ + 22 chars + '_' + 59 chars.

detectors.name: gitlab-pat
detectors.description: GitLab personal access token: glpat- + 20 chars.

detectors.name: google-api-key
detectors.description: Google API key: the fixed AIza prefix + 35 chars.

detectors.name: slack-webhook
detectors.description: Slack incoming-webhook URL; the team/channel/token path segments are the secret (the host is kept).

detectors.name: stripe-secret-key
detectors.description: Stripe secret/restricted key (sk_/rk_ live or test). Publishable pk_ keys are intentionally NOT matched (not secret).

detectors.name: twilio-api-key
detectors.description: Twilio API key SID (SK + 32 hex). The AC… account SID is an identifier, not a secret, and is not matched.

detectors.name: npm-token
detectors.description: npm access token: npm_ + 36 base62 chars.

detectors.name: auth-bearer
detectors.description: Credential following an HTTP 'Bearer ' scheme (Authorization header / curl logs). The scheme word is kept; the token is redacted.

detectors.name: auth-basic
detectors.description: base64 credential following an HTTP 'Basic ' scheme (it decodes to user:password).

detectors.name: conn-string-url-password
detectors.description: Password embedded in a URL's userinfo, e.g. postgres://user:PASS@host. Only the password (between ':' and '@') is redacted; a host:port with no '@' is left alone.

detectors.name: conn-string-password
detectors.description: Password value in a key=value connection string (Password=...; / pwd=...).

detectors.name: secret-assignment
detectors.description: Generic 'key = value' secret assignment (api_key/secret/token/password/...): the value (>=8 chars) is redacted. Broadest detector here — opt-in by design.
